The Agentic
SDLC Platform

An AI agent across the entire development lifecycle. Owned and operated by SQLI.

April 2026

The Problem

Every new engagement
starts the same way

  • — Infrastructure provisioned by hand, differently each time
  • — Repositories, CI/CD, and testing set up from scratch
  • — Monitoring and security added as an afterthought
  • — Sprint planning, docs, and status reports: all manual

The hidden cost

This doesn't just happen at setup. The manual overhead continues throughout the engagement: sprint admin, status reports, documentation, infrastructure changes.

The result

Manual, inconsistent, and impossible to scale. Quality depends on who's available, not on what the firm knows.

The Solution

Every engagement starts with an agent.

A platform that runs on three planes. SQLI engineers operate on the first; targeted automations run on the second; agent teams take on bounded batches on the third. Same skills, same knowledge, three ways to deliver.

Plane 1 — Engineering

Engineers with Claude Code

Creative, exploratory, judgment-rich work. Brand systems, code, decisions, documents, provisioning new engagements. Interactive, iterative, low-latency.

Plane 2 — Automation

Atomic tasks on triggers

Cron- and webhook-driven. Status reports, PR reviews, ticket triage, alert handling. Reliable, auditable, no human in the loop.

Plane 3 — Agent Teams

Bounded batches autonomously

Page generation, test generation, content at volume. Specialist agents in a loop with shared memory. Engineer kicks off, reviews at checkpoints.

Strategic Positioning

AI powers delivery,
not the product catalog

Integrated methodology, institutional knowledge, cross-engagement learning. Encoded in skills and knowledge, not sold as a feature. That's what competitors cannot copy.

Vendors already offer

  • GitHub Copilot ships with Enterprise
  • Atlassian Intelligence is built into Jira
  • Xray offers AI test generation
Value Proposition

Three audiences, three returns

For SQLI

  • Reduce manual overhead across the full engagement
  • Free senior engineers for delivery
  • Institutional knowledge stays with the firm
  • Platform compounds: each client improves it

For Clients

  • Environment ready quickly, with full quality from the start
  • Best practices from day one
  • Agent supports the team throughout
  • Benefits from all SQLI engagements

For Dev Teams

  • Infra and testing ready on arrival
  • Choose how much to use the agent
  • AI handles scaffolding, docs, tests
  • Focus on architecture and business logic
What "Agentic" Means

Knows. Reports. Creates. Operates. Acts.

What the engagement agent actually does — and the boundary of its autonomy at each level.

Knows the project

Read-only · institutional memory

Architecture decisions, conventions, and project history available on demand. "Why did we choose this stack?" — the agent reads the relevant ADR, summarizes the alternatives evaluated, and the date the decision was made.

Reports the state

Read-only · cross-system synthesis

Live state of delivery, infrastructure, and quality, synthesized across tools. "How is this sprint going?" — the agent cross-references Jira, GitHub, and observability data into a delivery snapshot with risks flagged.

Creates artifacts

Generative · multi-modal output

Brand systems from physical assets, mid-fidelity designs, copy, image-generation prompts, migrated templates, refactored modules. The agent doesn't only orchestrate — it produces deliverables, drafts the work, and hands it back for human iteration.

Operates routine work

Low-blast · auditable

Repetitive work the team would do anyway, executed consistently and logged. Weekly status reports posted to Confluence. New tickets triaged on arrival. Build failures diagnosed in pull request comments. Pull requests pre-reviewed for convention compliance.

Acts with authority

Human-in-the-loop · gated

Privileged actions — production deploys, scaling, migrations, schema changes. The agent prepares the change, presents the impact and the risk, then waits for explicit human approval. Enforcement is architectural, not instructional — gates live in the MCP / API layer with per-operation token scoping. The runtime cannot bypass them by deciding to.

Active at every stage — planning, building, testing, deploying, monitoring, documenting. Capabilities expand by adding skills, not by retraining the agent.

Engagement Modes

Where the agent operates

Two ways to engage. SQLI hosts the toolchain end-to-end, or the agent embeds inside the client's existing stack. The agent's capabilities are the same; only the surface it operates on differs.

Managed

SQLI hosts the toolchain

Client onboards into SQLI’s tooling — Atlassian, GitHub Enterprise, Azure, Datadog. The agent operates the full lifecycle on infrastructure SQLI owns and operates centrally. End-to-end delivery, consistent quality floor, low setup overhead.
Embedded

Agent inside the client’s stack

The agent operates inside the client’s existing infrastructure and tooling, adapting to their conventions. SQLI provides capacity and AI augmentation; the client retains platform sovereignty. Best fit for clients with mature dev teams and their own DevOps stack.

An engagement can shift mode over time — embedded engagements can graduate to managed if the client moves to SQLI’s tooling.

Developer Experience

Your choice. Your pace.

The agent is a permanent member of the team. How much developers lean on it is their choice.

Full Agentic

Work through the engagement team

Planning, coding, testing, docs, deployments — delegated to the agent team with full project context.

Hybrid

Code your way, delegate the rest

Write code in your IDE. The agent handles SDLC glue: sprint planning, Jira sync, docs, infrastructure.

Minimal

Use skills on demand

Invoke specific skills when you need them. The agent is a tool, not a partner.

All three modes work on the same platform. Adoption is gradual.

Operating Model

Three planes of operation

Each workload picks the plane that fits. The substrate is shared; the operator and the technology are not.

The Substrate — shared across all planes

Skills · ADRs · Brand · MCPs · Conventions · Per-client context. Versioned in Git. The investment that compounds.

↑ consumed by
↑ consumed by
↑ consumed by

Plane 1 · Engineering

Claude Code

Operator: SQLI engineer
Pattern: interactive, iterative
For: brand, code, ADRs, decisions, provisioning (/new-client), kickoff

Plane 2 · Automation

Managed Agents — single-shot

Operator: trigger (cron, webhook)
Pattern: atomic, predictable, autonomous
For: status reports, PR reviews, ticket triage, alert handling

Plane 3 · Agent Team

Managed Agents — multi-agent loop

Operator: engineer kicks off, autonomous after
Pattern: bounded batch, multi-step per item
For: page generation, test generation, content at volume

Three questions decide the plane: does it need an engineer’s judgment in the loop? Is it a bounded action triggered by an event? Is it a batch of similar items needing multi-step processing? Each workload has one right answer.

Plane 3 In Action

Agent teams: from a batch to a delivery

An engineer prepares inputs and acceptance criteria. The team runs autonomously for hours. Outputs flow to Git, Confluence, and the engagement’s memory. Humans review at checkpoints, not at every step.

1 · Engineer prepares the batch

Pushes test inventory, framework conventions, coverage targets, and known-flake history to the engagement’s Memory Store. Defines the goal: “Generate or refresh tests for these 40 user stories. Run the suite. Classify fails. Suggest fixes.”

↓ kicks off

2 · Specialist team operates in a loop

A lead agent reads the queue, dispatches specialists per stage, reassembles the result.

TestWriter

E2E from user stories

UnitWriter

Unit tests from code

TestExecutor

Runs suite, captures fails

FailClassifier

Flaky vs. real

FixSuggestor

Patterns of known fixes

↓ per item

3 · Outputs flow to durable systems

GitHub commit (new and updated tests) · Memory Store update (test inventory, flake history) · Confluence test report (coverage and classification visible to stakeholders). Loop continues to the next user story.

↓ every N items

4 · Checkpoint — engineer reviews

Session pauses, posts a digest. Engineer approves, steers, or stops. The loop resumes from the next item if green-lit. Hours of work, minutes of human attention.

Same pattern fits content production at volume, code migration at volume, page generation. Where Claude Code hits its ceiling — long autonomous loops with shared state — agent teams take over.

Industrialization

Ownership, not templates

In Managed mode, SQLI owns and operates the toolchain centrally. Consistency comes from shared infrastructure, a shared agent, and one source of truth.

Project Management

SQLI's Atlassian

Managed clients on SQLI's Jira + Confluence.
Shared templates, cross-project visibility.

Source Control

SQLI's GitHub Enterprise

Code in SQLI's org by default.
Shared workflows, security, branch policies.

Infrastructure

SQLI's Azure

Infra on SQLI's subscriptions.
Terraform modules, managed identities.

Monitoring

SQLI's Datadog

Observability centralized.
Dashboards, APM, alerting.

Embedded engagements operate against the client's existing tooling — covered next.

Skills

Executable best practices

Provisioning

/new-client

Full environment + engagement team in a single command: infra, CI/CD, project mgmt, monitoring, agent roles.

Delivery

/sprint-planner

Reads epics, decomposes into sprint-ready Jira tasks with acceptance criteria.

Infrastructure

/terraform-module

Generates Terraform from architecture decisions. Follows SQLI patterns.

Creative

/brand-from-assets

Brand system and design tokens extracted from physical assets — packaging, signage, photography.

Migration

/site-migration

Adapts SaaS templates (TiendaNube, Shopify, VTEX) to a new design while keeping the platform live.

Creative

/content-suite

Copy, prompts for image generation, and structured content drafted against the brand system.

Documentation

/sqli-document

Branded Word documents — proposals, SOWs, reports. Generated from project context.

Reporting

/status-report

Weekly status from live Jira, GitHub, and repo data. No manual aggregation.

Strategy

/project-soul

Strategic advisor: vision, risk, competitive positioning, decision framework.

What Gets Provisioned

What /new-client creates

Three layers, one command: a team of agents, a working environment, and a creative foundation ready to be filled.

The Engagement Team

  • Persistent Engagement Agent
  • Specialized roles: writer, reviewer, brand guardian, visual validator, code migrator
  • MCPs scoped to this engagement
  • Skills library mounted from the SQLI knowledge repo

Infrastructure & Tooling

  • Azure: Container Apps, PostgreSQL, Key Vault, VNet
  • GitHub repo with branch protection and CODEOWNERS
  • CI/CD with SonarCloud quality gates
  • Jira Scrum board + Confluence space
  • Datadog dashboards, APM, alerts
  • Test scaffolding: Playwright, Jest, Xray
  • Entra ID principals, Key Vault policies

Creative & Knowledge Foundation

  • Engagement CLAUDE.md with project context
  • ADR-001 capturing initial architectural decisions
  • Brand system scaffolding, ready for client assets
  • Design tokens and component library template
  • Prompt library: copy, content, image generation
  • Auto-generated kickoff Confluence page

Run from Plane 1 by a SQLI engineer with Claude Code. The engagement team and the environment exist before the kickoff meeting ends — the project starts on the work, not on the setup.

Roadmap

Four phases

Week 1 Week 14
TBD
In Progress

Phase 1: Foundation

The substrate. Repo, skills, MCPs, brand, strategic docs. Plane 1 fully operational with Claude Code. Proof by practice.

Next

Phase 2: Codify + First Instances

Codify Muta skills into the substrate. First Plane 2 automation (status report). First Plane 3 agent team (page generation). Each plane proves itself with one real instance.

Phase 3: First Client (Muta)

Plane 1 and validated automations support planning, creative work, code, testing, docs while the storefront still runs on TiendaNube. SQLI-managed environment provisioned at migration cutover.

Phase 4: Scale

Second client. Cross-engagement learning. Multi-user external access. Each plane expands by demand, not by speculation.
